Resolution / Justification: Set ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 1 on both functions. Both are CloudFormation custom resources invoked synchronously once at create and once at delete — concurrency of 1 matches actual execution pattern.
Added VersioningConfiguration: Status: Enabled. Low-risk bucket with a single immutable object but zero-cost to enable.

Finding Title: Secrets Manager secret not encrypted with KMS CMK
Resolution / Justification: Secret is encrypted at rest using the default aws/secretsmanager service key. A customer-managed CMK adds key management overhead (rotation policy key policy cross-account considerations) with no security benefit for a single DCV desktop password in a trial deployment. The secret is scoped to a single stack and accessed only by the head node IAM role. CMK encryption is appropriate for production deployments with compliance requirements around key custody.

Bucket contains a single bootstrap shell script written once at stack creation by a Lambda custom resource. No user-facing read/write access. Access logging requires a separate destination bucket adding resource overhead for a bucket with effectively zero access surface. The bucket already enforces encryption (AES256) full public access block and deny-insecure-transport policy.

Both roles are single-purpose Lambda execution roles with tightly scoped inline policies. Inline policies are appropriate here because the policies have no reuse case — they exist solely for these Lambda functions and are deleted with the stack. Converting to managed policies would add template resources with no security improvement.

Both functions are CloudFormation custom resources invoked synchronously via cfnresponse. CloudFormation waits for the response and handles failures through stack rollback. There is no asynchronous invocation path so a DLQ would never receive messages. Adding a DLQ and associated SQS queue would be dead infrastructure.

Both functions call only public AWS API endpoints (S3 and EC2). They do not access any VPC-internal resources. Placing them in a VPC would require a NAT Gateway (~$32/month) or VPC endpoints adding cost and deployment complexity with no security benefit. This rule targets functions accessing private resources (RDS ElastiCache etc.) not utility functions calling public AWS APIs.
